item.title: loved it
item.content:encoded: I was reaally into this more than i expected and that last entry hit hard and i like that he stands by his stance more than anything

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is some heavy stuff, man. Instantly relatable to anyone who's had to deal with mood disorders. Well written, and packs a punch. +1.

item.title: The final log
item.content:encoded: I seriously appreciate all the feedback and suggestions as I've been tinkering with stuff; they've been immensely helpful. Added a short log after he discovers the butterflies, and made a few other small adjustments - most notably "haven't been home in" -> "haven't seen my family in". Hopefully that nudges the ending toward earned without tipping the balance back into predictable.

Loved this! Really made me uncomfortable and unsettled throughout and had a great storyline leading up to the final entry. I understand that it comes out of left field, but I personally loved it. Tied into him not seeing his family in the earlier entry nicely and the "That's what SCP-3209 took from me" really hit me for some reason. But I would agree that some more build up would have been a bit more sufficient to make this better, even though it's already a great skip.

+1, man. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fuck. I was enthralled with the concept, and I was loving what I thought was his slow descent into becoming one of the monks; the characterization is hella solid.

That last log broke it for me though. The son comes out of nowhere; he hasn’t mentioned a family once prior to it.

I could see foreshadowing it in the early logs (an offhanded mention to sending letters back home, something later about “when was the last time I wrote them?”) or you’ve already got a perfect set-up to cut the last log completely and just have him defect and become a monk.

Two ideas I had, I love like 90% of this so great job anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved this in the Drafts forum, and I still love it here. It's just unsettling for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this!

I anticipated the conclusion since I read the #1 Dad comment, though I seem to be unique in that. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've tried to touch up the entries so the last one comes as less of a shock, hopefully without losing its suddenness.

I'm glad you pointed this out, because I really want to get the ending right. My deepest fear is losing interest in the people I love, but there are plenty of traditions that not only encourage but demand severing emotional ties to your family. That was the tension I wanted to get at with the article, particularly since it suggests that having the power to define fear can come dangerously close to cultural imperialism. I do want it to be somewhat shocking, because it comes as a complete shock to Agent Bartell, but I don't want it to feel like a random bonus thrown into the story because the whole story is supposed to center on that moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was really with this until the final entry, which came completely out of left field, having no actual connection to the rest of the story, and came off as a cheap attempt to invoke pathos. -1

item.title: Re: Tentative +1
item.content:encoded: The butterfly isn't causing the guy to recommend against containing it. It took his interest in his kid, not his interest in containing butterflies.

item.title: Tentative +1
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Tiam_Kara that the last journal entry is a bit too on the nose for my liking. I think it'd be a lot better if you remove that one and trust your reader to realize the butterfly is causing the guy to recommend against containing it.
I did have some issues with the clinical tone here and there, but the story and idea here is good enough to forgive that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to have a no-vote but after I read the addendum I loved it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The descent to indifference here is most appropriate.
Didn't get to let you know in chat that I liked this. The very last entry was a bit heavy-handed last I read it but not enough to affect my vote.

item.title: With thanks to
item.content:encoded:
• The Great Hippo for feedback, advice, and a fantastic eye for detail
  • not_a_seagull for forum feedback
  • #site19 for checking it over
  • PublicDomainPictures for the image base
  • And viewers like you!
